text:
Russian and Belarusian athletes who won medals at the Olympics should be stripped of them after failing to adhere to rules, a human rights group has claimed.

Global Rights Compliance (GRC) had claimed in the lead up to the gamesthat more than half of those set to appear had failed to adhere to rules imposed by theInternational Olympic Committee (IOC)on which their participation as neutral athletes hinged.

Athletes competing as neutrals were prohibited from demonstrating support for theRussian warinUkraineand from having any contact with military or national security agencies, but GRC claims that this was ignored by many of those taking part.

31RussianandBelarusianathletes competed as neutral athletes with five going on to win medals in four events.

Wayne Jordash KC, President of Global Rights Compliance, said, “These Olympic Games were an opportunity for the IOC to affirm its commitments to peace, stability and human rights for all.

READ MORE:Ukrainian Olympian's fury as Russian athletes at Paris 'feeding Putin's war'



“Instead, despite receiving evidence showing that many athletes had contravened these principles, it buried its head in the sand.”

GRC intends to lobby the IOC to strip the athletes of their medals, with the group claiming that they have damming evidence that all of the winners failed to adhere to the rules.

Jeremy Pizzi, Legal Advisor Global Rights Compliance, said: “Ivan Litvinovich, the trampolinist who won gold has appeared at pro-military events in Belarus since his selection to compete.

“The others have shown support the war on social media, which despite most of their accounts being scrubbed since the invasion, we still have evidence of them doing so.”

Don't miss:Andy Murray has already hinted at 'exciting' next job after retiring at Olympics

Don't miss:Olympics golf commentator apologises for 'inappropriate' slip of the tongue

Don't miss:Team GB runner finished Olympic marathon with broken leg



Mr Pizzi expressed disbelief that despite being made aware of the flagrant violation of the rules, Russian and Belarusian athletes were still able to compete.

He said: “It is a disgrace that Ukrainian athletes are forced to compete against people who in another scenario, would praise their killing for wearing the country’s flag.”

The group believes that the stripping of the medal winners is an important step, especially if the IOC is to maintain credibility ahead of the Paralympics set to get underway in a fortnight.

Mr Pizzi said: “We could potentially encounter the ridiculous situation where Ukrainian Paralympians, who are disabled as a result of the war, are having to compete against people that support the conflict that caused their injuries.

“How can the IOC and their sponsors justify this?”



The group is pessimistic of their chances, with the IOC refusing to respond to them for many months but believe that pressuring the organisation and the sponsors that fund them is crucial in holding to account an organisation that speaks often about respect from human rights.

Mr Pizzi said: “The IOC’s supposed ‘commitments’ to human rights are nothing more than window dressing for an organisation that shamefully refuses to take accountability.”

The IOC did not respond to a request for comment.

title: Putin's Olympic heroes 'should be stripped of their ill-gotten medals' | World | News | Express.co.uk
author: Conor Wilson
description: Russian and Belarusian  Olympic medal winners should never have been allowed to compete after breaking eligibility rules, a human rights group has claimed.
